results.title: Live resin vs live rosin explanation
results.response:
Hey team! Got a great question from a customer - let me break down the difference between live resin and live rosin.

Live resin and live rosin are both made from fresh-frozen plant material, which helps preserve more terpenes than traditional extracts made from dried flower. The key difference is in the extraction method:

Live resin uses a chemical solvent (typically butane or hydrocarbons) to extract cannabinoids and terpenes from the fresh-frozen flower.

Live rosin is completely solventless - made by applying heat and pressure to ice water hash (bubble hash) that was also made from fresh-frozen flower. There are zero solvents in the final product.

Important to note: Live rosin does NOT come from live resin. They are separate products with separate processes. The word "live" in both names refers to using fresh-frozen starting material, not a relationship between the two products.

Live rosin typically commands a premium price due to the additional processing steps and solventless nature.

How are we positioning these in our menu highlights right now? Any questions customers seem to have about these products?

results.title: Live resin vs live rosin — process only
results.response:
Hey there! I'm checking our budtender education sheet to make sure I give you the straight scoop on live resin vs live rosin.

Here's the breakdown for your team:

Live resin is made with solvents - specifically butane or hydrocarbons. The process starts with fresh-frozen plant material and uses those solvents to extract the concentrate. It's in the premium BHO category and known for being aromatic and terpene-rich.

Live rosin is completely solventless. It's made by applying heat and pressure to bubble hash (which comes from ice water extraction) made from fresh-frozen flower. So no solvents touch the product at any step - just heat and pressure.

The key differentiator for your budtenders to remember: "Both start fresh-frozen. Live resin = solvents (like butane). Live rosin = no solvents - just heat and pressure. That's why rosin costs more."
